coredata.dc:title: Selective supramolecular interaction of ethylenediamine functionalized graphene quantum dots: Ultra-sensitive photoluminescence detection for nickel ion in vitro
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Anli
He, Peng
Huang, Tao
Li, Jiurong
Hu, Xurui
Xiang, Pengcheng
Chen, Da
Yang, Siwei
Wang, Gang
Ding, Guqiao
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots have attracted the researchers' attention due to its high stability, low cost and controllable photoluminescence performance. In this paper, we report the synthesis of ethylenediamine functionalized graphene quantum dots (E-GQDs). The E-GQDs is synthesized via hydrothermal treatment of the mixed solution of graphene quantum dots and ethylenediamine. The E-GQDs shows remarkable quantum yield (0.83). The quantum yield of E-GQDs is higher than quantum yields of most previously reported GQDs. Moreover, due to the coordination between Ni2+ and ethylenediamine, the PL of E-GQDs quenched via static quenching process. This makes the E-GQDs an alternative for ultra-sensitive PL probe of Ni2+ (detection limit: 3 × 10−8 M) with high antijamming capability. We also demonstrated the good performance of E-GQDs in vitro detection of Ni2+.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Photoluminescence
Photoluminescence detection
Sensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (Nos. 11704204 , 11774368 and 61604084 , and 11704010 ), General Financial Grant from China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Nos. 2017M621564 and BX201700271 ), K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University and the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo under Grant (No. 2017A610104 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of E-GQDs. (b) Size distribution histogram of GQDs and E-GQDs. (c) HR-TEM image, (d) FFT, (e) Raman spectrum and (f) AFM image of E-GQDs. (g) Core level O 1 s XPS spectra of GQDs and E-GQDs. Core level (h) C 1 s and (i) N 1 s XPS spectra of E-GQDs. (j) Schematic diagram showing the structure of E-GQDs.

(a) Normalized Uv–vis, PLE and PL spectra of E-GQDs (inset: photograph of 1.0 mg/mL E-GQDs aqueous solution under 365 nm UV light). (b) A brief summary of Em and φ of GQDs. (c) PL decay curve of E-GQDs. (d) PL spectra of E-GQDs recorded for the progressively longer EX of 20 nm increments (450–570 nm). (e) Influence of different ions or biomolecules on the PL intensity change of E-GQDs under room temperature. F and F0 is the PL intensity of E-GQDs with and without the present of different ions or biomolecules, respectively. The concentration of ions and biomolecules is 0.1 M and 05 mM, respectively. The concentration of E-GQDs is 1 mg/mL.

(a) PL spectra of E-GQDs (0.5 mg/mL) different [Ni2+] (0–50 μM). (b) Stern-Volmer plot versus different [Ni2+]. (c) PL spectra of the mixture of E-CQD and Ni2+ with and without the present of EDTA ([Ni2+] = 10 μM, the concentration of EDTA and E-GQDs is 10 μM and 0.5 mg/mL, respectively) under room temperature. (d) The τ of E-GQDs under different [Ni2+] and room temperature.

(a) Metabolic activity of rADSCs cells with different concentrations of E-GQDs. (b–d) live/dead assay of rADSCs cells after E-GQDs treatment (200 μg/mL, 24 h). (b) merge, (c) live, (d) dead. Confocal fluorescence microphotograph of rADSCs (loaded with 1.0 mL, 50 μg/mL, E-GQDs aqueous solution for 2 h) (e) without (f) with the present of Ni2+ (5 μM).

head.ce:title: Selective supramolecular interaction of ethylenediamine functionalized graphene quantum dots: Ultra-sensitive photoluminescence detection for nickel ion in vitro

Shanghai Institute of microsystem and Information Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots have attracted the researchers' attention due to its high stability, low cost and controllable photoluminescence performance. In this paper, we report the synthesis of ethylenediamine functionalized graphene quantum dots (E-GQDs). The E-GQDs is synthesized via hydrothermal treatment of the mixed solution of graphene quantum dots and ethylenediamine. The E-GQDs shows remarkable quantum yield (0.83). The quantum yield of E-GQDs is higher than quantum yields of most previously reported GQDs. Moreover, due to the coordination between Ni2+ and ethylenediamine, the PL of E-GQDs quenched via static quenching process. This makes the E-GQDs an alternative for ultra-sensitive PL probe of Ni2+ (detection limit: 3 × 10−8 M) with high antijamming capability. We also demonstrated the good performance of E-GQDs in vitro detection of Ni2+.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new kind of carbon based semiconductor, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) attracted the researchers' attention due to its high stability [1], low cost [2] and controllable photoluminescence (PL) performance [3]. Moreover, as a carbon based material, GQDs avoid heavy metal pollution risk in practical applications (by contrast, traditional semiconductor quantum dots such as CdS and CdSe show high heavy metal pollution risk in practical applications), which makes GQDs a kind of environmentally friendly functional material and biocompatible materials [4–14]. By now, diversified in vitro/vivo functional device have been developed based on GQDs [15,16]. Sun et al. demonstrated the high application value of N doped GQDs in fluorescence bio-imaging [17]. Ding et al. reported selenium doped GQDs (Se-GQDs) based fluorescent switch for the detection of oxidative hydroxyl radical and reductive glutathione in vitro [18]. The PL of Se-GQDs was quenched by hydroxyl radical which caused the fluorescence switch to “off” state [18]. The PL recovered as the “on” state with the present of glutathione [18]. Such PL switch provides a new detection approach for redox events involved in cellular redox regulation [18]. Although various GQDs based PL probes and PL imaging agents have been developed, new PL probes with high sensitivity and selectivity are still required [19].

Nickel (Ni) is a kind of widely used metal (such as high performance alloys and corrosion-resistant decorative platings) [20,21]. However, prolonged contact with Ni may cause human skin irritation and allergic reactions. Moreover, Ni2+ is one of the potential pathogenic factors of leukemia [22]. Determination of serum nickel levels can be used as an auxiliary indicator for the diagnosis of leukemia and can be used to estimate the condition and predict the trend of change [23]. Thus, it is very important to develop Ni2+ PL probe with high sensitivity and selectivity.

In this paper, we report the synthesis of ethylenediamine functionalized graphene quantum dots (E-GQDs). The E-GQDs which were obtained via hydrothermal treatment of the mixed solution of GQDs and ethylenediamine. The E-GQDs shows remarkable PL properties (the quantum yield is as high as 0.83 and higher than most previously reported GQDs), stability and biocompatibility. Moreover, due to the coordination between Ni2+ and ethylenediamine, the PL of E-GQDs quenched via static quenching process. This makes the E-GQDs can be used as an ultra-sensitive PL probe for the detection of Ni2+ (detection limit: 3 × 10−8 M) with high antijamming capability. Based on above results, we finally demonstrated that the E-GQDs can be used in vitro detection of Ni2+.

H2O2 (30 wt.%), graphene oxide (GO, 98%), ethylenediamine (AR), Ni(NO3)2 (AR), LiCl (AR), NaCl (AR), KCl (AR), MgCl2 (AR), CaCl2 (AR), BaCl2 (AR), Al(NO3)3 (AR), CoCl2 (AR), MnCl2 (AR), CuCl2 (AR), ZnCl2 (AR), FeCl2 (AR), Fe(NO3)3 (AR), CdCl2 (AR), HgCl2 (AR), NaBr (AR), NaOH (AR), Na2SO4 (AR), NaNO2 (AR) and other chemicals were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China) and used as received without further purification. The water used throughout all experiments was purified using a Millipore system. Transmission electron microscopy (Hitachi H-8100) was carried out to determine the morphologies of the E-GQDs. Atomic force microscope (Oxford Instruments, Cypher S) was utilized to determine the size and thickness of the E-GQDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (PHI Quantera II system, Ulvac-PHI, INC, Japan) was utilized to determine the surface chemical composition and chemical states of the E-GQDs. The ultraviolet-visible absorption properties of E-GQD were characterized by a UV-5800 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation spectra were collected on a PerkinElmer LS55 luminescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer Instruments, U.K.) at room temperature. Raman spectra (HORIBA Jobin Yvon HR800) were obtained using an Ar+ laser with the wavelength of 514 nm.

Quantum yield (φ) measurements: Rhodamine B in ethanol (φ = 0.69) was chosen as a standard for E-GQDs. The quantum yield of E-GQDs was calculated according to Eq. (1) [20].
(1)
φ =
φ R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
η 2
η R 2

Where φ is the quantum yield, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, η is the refractive index of the solvent, A is the optical density, and the subscript R refers to the reference standard with a known quantum yield. In order to minimize reabsorption effects, absorbance in the 10 mm fluorescence cuvette was kept under 0.1 at 360 nm excitation wavelength.

The rADSCs culture were obtained from subcutaneous adipose tissue in the inguinal groove of 6-week-old male Sprague Dawley rats (Shanghai Animal Experimental Center, China) and cultured in F12/DMEM (Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Media: Nutrient Mixture F-12) supplemented with 10% FBS (Invitrogen) and 100 units/mL penicillin-streptomycin (Invitrogen) according to our protocol.

The typical preparation process of E-GQDs is as follows. First, GQDs (precursor of E-GQDs) with oxygen-containing groups were prepared. 5.0 mL of 30 mg mL−1 GO aqueous solution was added into 5.0 mL of 2.0 M (6 wt.%) H2O2 aqueous solution. Then, 50 mg W18O49 nanowires [24] were added into above mixture. Such mixture was transferred into a 15 mL Teflons-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 96 h [25]. The pale yellow GQDs aqueous solution was obtained. Next, 100 mg ethylenediamine was added into 10.0 mL of 5 mg mL−1 GQDs aqueous solution. The mixture was transferred into a 15 mL Teflons-lined autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 24 h. The reaction solution was further freeze-dried and the E-GQDs powder was obtained.

Fig. 1 a shows the transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of E-GQDs. Homogeneous E-GQDs with average size of about 4.2 nm can be observed. The average size of E-GQDs is larger than GQDs (3.8 nm, Fig. 1b, Fig. S1). This can be due to the edge modification of GQDs by ethylenediamine. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image of E-GQDs (Fig. 1c) shows the crystalline structure with lattice spacing of 0.24 nm which can be attributed to the (110) diffraction planes of graphene [26]. Fast Fourier transform (FFT, Fig. 1d) shows a significant standard six-fold symmetry, indicating the high crystalline structure of the obtained E-GQDs [27]. Raman spectrum of E-GQDs thus formed is shown in Fig. 1e. The peak located around 1584-1589 cm−1 corresponds to the in-plane vibrations of the sp2 graphene lattice (G band). The peak located around 1337-1346 cm−1 can be associated with the breathing mode of the sp3 C atoms (D band) [28]. The intensity ratio of the D to G band (ID/IG) is an important indicator for evaluating the structure and quality of carbon materials. The ID/IG of E-GQDs thus formed is 0.95. This indicates the good crystalline nature of as-prepared E-GQDs, which further confirmed the TEM results. Atomic force microscope (AFM) observation (Fig. 1f) reveals highly dispersed E-GQDs on SiO2 substrate with a typical topographic height of 0.5–2 nm. This indicates that most E-GQDs consist of ca. 1–4 graphene layers [29].

The structure of E-GQDs is further characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). The XPS survey spectra (Fig. S2) of the precursor (GQDs) and E-GQDs both show a predominant graphitic C 1 s peak at 285.0 eV and an O 1 s peak at 531.7 eV [30]. The O/C atomic ratio of GQDs and E-GQDs is 0.55 and 0.32, respectively (Fig. S3) [31]. The oxygen content of E-GQDs is lower than that of GQDs. This can be due to the condensation reaction between OH groups of GQDs and ethylenediamine. Moreover, an obvious N 1 s peak (400.0 eV) can be observed in the XPS survey spectrum of E-GQDs [32]. This indicates the successful introduction of ethylenediamine.

Core level XPS spectra (O 1 s, N 1 s and C 1 s) of E-GQDs are shown in Fig. 1h-i. Fig. 1h shows the O 1 s spectra of GQDs and E-GQDs. The peak located at 531.08 and 528.59 eV can be ascribed to the CO and CO, respectively [33,34]. It’s clear that the content of CO reduced after surface modification. This is due to the condensation reaction between COH groups and ethylenediamine. Fig. 1i shows the C 1 s spectrum of E-GQDs, the peak located at 284.94, 286.81, 287.24 and 288.61 eV can be assigned to the CC/CC, COH, CN/CN and CO groups [35]. Moreover, the N 1 s spectrum of E-GQDs further confirmed above results. As shown in Fig. 1i, the peak located at 399.48 and 401.21 eV stem from the CN and CN, respectively [36,37]. Based on above results, the structure of E-GQDs is shown in Fig. 1j. Successful surface modification of GQDs results in the significant change of surface state. The zeta potential of E-GQDs is −37.8 mV, which is much higher than that of GQDs (−14.7 mV).

Optical properties of E-GQDs are further characterized by Uv–vis absorption spectra, PL spectra and PL decay spectrum. Fig. 2 a shows the Uv–vis absorption spectrum of E-GQDs aqueous solution. The Uv–vis absorption spectrum of E-GQDs shows a typical peak at 290 nm which indicates the π-π* transition model of aromatic sp2 domains [38]. E-GQDs also show a long tail extending into the visible range. Photoluminescence excitation (PLE, red curve in Fig. 2a) spectrum of E-GQDs aqueous solution indicates the excitation wavelength (Ex) of E-GQDs is 505 nm. It's worth noting that the optimal excitation wavelength of E-GQDs corresponds to the band gap transition absorption peak (500 nm) in UV–vis absorption spectrum. This indicates the PL process of E-GQDs is interband transition process. The maximum emission wavelength (Em) of E-GQDs is 590 nm. The Stokes shift and full width half-maximum of E-GQDs is 85 and 110 eV, respectively. This indicates the E-GQDs have weak self-absorption effect and low energy loss. Moreover, no obvious change of Ex and Em can be observed between E-GQDs and GQDs (Fig. S4). This result suggests the weak interaction between ethylenediamine groups and graphene core.

The quantum yield (φ) of E-GQDs is 0.83, as shown in inset of Fig. 2a, and the E-GQDs aqueous solution shows bright yellow PL under Uv light. The φ of E-GQDs is much higher than that of GQDs (Fig. 2b) [36,32–73]. This may result from the surface passivation of ethylenediamine. Moreover, such ultrahigh φ of E-GQDs is higher than most reported GQDs, indicating the efficient interband transition process of E-GQDs.

PL decay of E-GQDs is measured by a time-correlated single photon counting technique and fitted well with a biexponential decay behavior. As shown in Fig. 2c, The PL lifetime (τ) of E-GQDs can be dominated by a long decay component of 4.2 ns (93%) with a small contribution from the short decay of 0.9 ns (7%). Thus, the weighted-average τ is 4.05 ns. The radiative rates (κr, κr = φ/τ) of E-GQDs is 2.05 × 108 s−1. Such high κr indicates the high electronic transition probability in E-GQDs [74].

E-GQDs show good stability. As shown in Figs. S5-6, no obvious change of PL intensity of E-GQDs can be observed under long time UV light irradiation (Xe lamp with center wavelength at 320 nm, 48 h) or visible light irradiation (450–850 nm, 60 days). This can be ascribed to the stable structure of E-GQDs. Moreover, due to the interband transition process, the E-GQDs show excitation wavelength independence PL. As shown in Fig. 2d, the Em show no obvious change when the Ex shift from 450 to 570 nm. Moreover, the E-GQDs also show stable PL with the present of ions (Li+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Co2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Cl−, Br−, OH−, SO4 2-, NO3 − and NO2 −) and amino acids (Fig. 2e). However, the PL of E-GQDs was strongly quenched by Ni2+. This indicates the E-GQDs can be used as specific PL probe for Ni2+ detection.

As shown in Fig. 3 a, the dramatic PL quenching behavior of E-GQDs can be observed when Ni2+ (Ni(NO3)2) is added. Obviously, the PL intensity of E-GQDs decreased with the increased Ni2+ concentration. The PL behavior follows the equation as follows [75,76]:
F0/F = 1+K[Ni2+]
where [Ni2+] is the Ni2+ concentration. F0 and F is the PL intensities of E-GQDs with the absence and presence of Ni2+, respectively. The plot presented in Fig. 3b shows good linear relationship in the [Ni2+] concentration range of 1 × 10−7 to 5 × 10-5 M. The correlation coefficient (r2) is 0.9953 determining [Ni2+] over the linear concentration range of 1 × 10−7 to 5 × 10-5 M. The detection limit of E-GQDs is estimated to be 3 × 10−8 M at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3.

The quenching mechanism of E-GQDs with the present of Ni2+ was further discussed. The combination mode between Ni2+ and E-GQDs may be the strong coordination interactions between Ni2+ and ethylenediamine groups. Thus, the ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid-2Na (EDTA) was introduced. As shown in Fig. 3c, the PL of E-GQDs was recovered with the present of EDTA. Moreover, the precursor (GQDs) shows no PL quenching behavior with the present of Ni2+ (Fig. S7). This further confirmed the above speculate [4].

PL lifetime measurements was carried out for further evidence of the quenching mechanism of E-GQDs. In static quenching, the τ of E-GQDs does not change (τ0/τ = 1, τ0 and τ are the τ in the absence and presence of quencher, respectively). In static quenching, E-GQDs does not form a complex with the quencher in the ground state having the τ0. On the other hand, in dynamic quenching, F0/F=τ0/τ, and the τ decreases with addition of the quencher. As shown in Fig. 3d, the τ of E-GQDs show no obvious change with the addition of Ni2+. The τ0/τ≈1 with the [Ni2+] ranging from 1 × 10−7 to 5 × 10-5 M. Based on above results, the sensing process of E-GQDs can be attributed to the supramolecular interaction between E-GQDs and Ni2+ [77,78]. Due to the coordination between Ni2+ and ethylenediamine, the PL of E-GQDs quenched via static quenching process.

E-GQDs can also be used in vitro detection of Ni2+. For a practical bioimaging application, the in vitro cytotoxicity of E-GQDs is evaluate by rADSCs cells. As shown in Fig. 4 a, metabolic activity of rADSCs cells was treated with different concentrations of E-GQDs. E-GQDs aqueous solution with different concentrations (0–500 μg/mL) were added into the rADSCs cells cultured in 96-well plates and incubated for 24 h. Next, the standard assay was carried out to assess the cell viabilities after the E-GQDs treatment [59]. No obvious reduction in cell viability can be observed after E-GQDs treatment even under high concentrations (up to 500 μg/mL). Moreover, the live/dead assay was then performed. As shown in Fig. 4b–d, the live cells stained green and the dead cells are shown in red. The viability of rADSCs cells kept above 98% after E-GQDs treatment (200 μg/mL, 24 h). These results demonstrated the E-GQDs treatment have no obvious toxic in vitro.

Finally, we show the detection of Ni2+ in rADSCs cells. As shown in Fig. 4e, bright yellow PL can be observed in rADSCs cells after E-GQDs were incubated with rADSCs cells (50 μg/mL, 2 h). This indicates the E-GQDs have been internalized by the rADSCs cells. However, when the Ni2+ was introduced (5 μM, 4 h), the PL of E-GQDs was quenched (Fig. 4f). Thus, the E-GQDs can be used in vitro detection of Ni2+.

In summary, we synthesized E-GQDs via the hydrothermal treatment of the mixed solution of GQDs and ethylenediamine. The obtained yellow emitted E-GQDs shows high quantum yield (0.83), excellent stability and biocompatibility. Moreover, due to the coordination between Ni2+ and ethylenediamine, the PL of E-GQDs quenched via static quenching process. This makes the E-GQDs an ultra-sensitive PL probe for the detection of Ni2+ with high antijamming capability. E-GQDs were demonstrated can be used in vitro detection of Ni2+, based on above results. This work is not only helpful for the further understanding of the metabolic process of Ni2+ in vitro, but also helpful for the development of GQDs based PL probes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (Nos. 11704204, 11774368 and 61604084, and 11704010), General Financial Grant from China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Nos. 2017M621564 and BX201700271), K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University and the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo under Grant (No. 2017A610104).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.synthmet.2018.05.013.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: J. Peng, W. Gao, B. K. Gupta, Z. Liu, R. Romero-Aburto, L. H. Ge, L. Song, L. B. Alemany, X. B. Z, G. H. Gao, S. A. Vithayathil, B. A. Kaipparettu, A. A. Marti, T. Hayashi, J. J. Zhu, P. M. Ajayan, Nano Lett. 12 (2012) 844–849.
X. Li, S. W. Yang, J. Sun, P. He, X. Xu, G. Q. Carbon 78 (2014) 38–48.
